.\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_15\codex_stderr.log:3780:outputs\campaigns\CAMP_20260310T073230_fb78bf\WEB_20260310T073230_5da0fc.json:2877:      "content": "The vadose zone functions as the critical interface determining PFAS fate prior to groundwater contamination, with chain-length-dependent partitioning governing whether compounds remain sequestered in soil or migrate downward. Moreover, this decoupling between total soil burden and leachable fraction creates fundamental uncertainty in risk assessment methodologies that rely on bulk soil measurements. Moreover, while interfacial sequestration at air-water boundaries temporarily retards vertical migration of long-chain compounds, this same mechanism creates a reservoir subject to mobilization upon water table fluctuations or infiltration events —a dynamic discussed in relation to secondary source formation in Sediment Contamination Hotspots: Formation and Secondary Release Dynamics. However, short-chain compounds, lacking comparable interfacial affinity, maintain higher effective mobility through the vadose profile despite lower individual sorption affinities per unit surface area. Sorption-desorption kinetics display pronounced hysteresis, with desorption rates systematically slower than sorption rates for both chain-length classes. Significantly, the magnitude of this kinetic asymmetry increases with perfluorocarbon chain length, reflecting stronger van der Waals interactions between fluorinated tails and soil surfaces —consistent with the chain-length-dependent stability patterns examined in Chemical Persistence and Environmental Stability. Long-chain PFAS demonstrate greater resistance to leaching under steady-state flow conditions but may exhibit delayed pulse releases when hydrological perturbations disrupt equilibrium conditions. Furthermore, this \"resistance to leaching\" refers specifically to retarded vertical transport rather than immobilization; long-chain compounds remain susceptible to episodic mobilization as addressed in discussions of persistent secondary sources. Short-chain compounds achieve substantial porewater concentrations due to higher initial aqueous solubility and more rapid desorption kinetics—properties examined in Surface Activity and Solubility Controls on Foam Formation and Stability—enabling faster breakthrough to underlying aquifers. The relationship between soil organic carbon content and PFAS retention follows modified partitioning models accounting for the amphiphilic nature of these contaminants. Moreover, unlike nonpolar organic compounds where sorption correlates linearly with organic carbon fraction, PFAS partitioning incorporates electrostatic interactions with mineral surfaces and specific binding to charged soil constituents. Long-chain perfluoroalkyl acids particularly exhibit this dual-mode sorption behavior, partitioning into organic carbon domains while simultaneously coordinating with metal oxide surfaces through their charged headgroups. In addition, short-chain analogs participate less effectively in hydrophobic partitioning, rendering their transport more sensitive to pH-dependent surface charge variations and ionic strength effects. Vadose zone processes preceding aquifer contamination include multiple retention mechanisms operating at distinct temporal scales. Immediate sorption to mineral surfaces occurs within hours, followed by slower diffusion into soil organic matter and intraparticle micropores over days to weeks. Consequently, long-chain PFAS penetrate these immobile domains more extensively due to favorable free energy of transfer, effectively removing them from mobile porewater fractions on timescales relevant to episodic infiltration events. Short-chain compounds remain predominantly in rapidly exchanging pools, maintaining higher effective mobility despite lower affinity per sorption site. Nevertheless, sites with historical long-chain PFAS releases may exhibit delayed groundwater impacts relative to short-chain contamination, not due to absence of source term but due to vadose zone retention capacity. Short-chain replacement compounds introduced after regulatory phase-outs demonstrate more rapid breakthrough to groundwater, potentially underestimating transport velocities calculated from conservative tracer behavior. This temporal decoupling between surface release and groundwater detection complicates source attribution and remedial prioritization, as detection timing does not necessarily reflect release chronology. In addition, treatment implications extend directly from these mechanistic understandings. Ex-situ soil washing and in-situ flushing technologies must account for chain-length-dependent desorption kinetics; long-chain PFAS require more aggressive chemical enhancement or extended residence times to achieve equivalent removal efficiency. Furthermore, the mobile porewater fraction—directly available for groundwater migration—contains disproportionate short-chain enrichment relative to total soil composition, suggesting that monitoring focused on leachable rather than total concentrations would better predict aquifer contamination risk. Remediation strategies targeting the vadose zone must incorporate chain-length-specific extraction protocols rather than assuming uniform contaminant behavior across PFAS classes. The capillary fringe region warrants particular attention as the transition zone where vadose zone processes intersect saturated groundwater systems. Moreover, long-chain PFAS accumulating at this interface through interfacial trapping mechanisms create concentrated transition zones that may serve as persistent source terms during water table rise events. Short-chain compounds, transit this boundary more readily due to limited retention capacity in the capillary fringe, establishing direct hydraulic connectivity between surface contamination and aquifer receptors with minimal attenuation. Additionally, soil moisture content emerges as a critical variable modulating effective partitioning coefficients across the unsaturated profile. At intermediate saturations, the competing effects of reduced aqueous phase volume and enhanced air-water interfacial area generate non-monotonic relationships between moisture state and PFAS mobility. Long-chain compounds exhibit maximum retention under partially saturated conditions where interfacial area is maximized, whereas complete saturation or near-dry conditions diminish this retention mechanism. Moreover, short-chain PFAS display comparatively muted moisture dependence, reflecting their dominant partitioning into the aqueous phase regardless of saturation state. The role of soil structure and aggregation in regulating pore-scale transport pathways remains underappreciated in conventional partitioning models. Furthermore, intra-aggregate porosity creates diffusion-limited domains where long-chain PFAS may become sequestered within soil structural units, physically isolating contaminants from advective flow paths. Short-chain compounds, with higher effective diffusion coefficients, more readily equilibrate between inter-aggregate and intra-aggregate porosity, maintaining concentration continuity across pore domains. This structural sequestration contributes to the apparent kinetic hysteresis observed in batch desorption experiments, conflating true chemical desorption resistance with physical transport limitations. Additionally, co-solute effects and competitive displacement phenomena introduce additional complexity in multi-component PFAS mixtures characteristic of AFFF formulations. The presence of short-chain compounds may indirectly facilitate long-chain mobilization through competitive displacement from high-affinity sorption sites, particularly under dynamic flow conditions where local equilibrium assumptions fail. Furthermore, long-chain PFAS at elevated concentrations may modify air-water interfacial properties, potentially altering the effective retention capacity for co-occurring short-chain analogs. These mixture-specific behaviors challenge the predictive capacity of single-component partitioning parameters applied to complex environmental systems. Temperature fluctuations through seasonal cycles or climate variability introduce thermodynamic perturbations to equilibrium partitioning relationships. Nevertheless, the exothermic nature of hydrophobic partitioning interactions implies enhanced retention at lower temperatures, with long-chain PFAS exhibiting greater temperature sensitivity due to larger enthalpic contributions from perfluorocarbon tail interactions. Short-chain compounds, with weaker overall sorption enthalpies, demonstrate more stable partitioning behavior across temperature ranges relevant to vadose zone environments. Additionally, seasonal groundwater recharge events coinciding with temperature minima may therefore mobilize differentially enriched PFAS signatures compared to warm-season infiltration. Conventional leaching tests performed under standard temperature and moisture conditions may systematically misrepresent field-scale mobilization potential, particularly for long-chain compounds where retention capacity varies nonlinearly with environmental state variables. Monitoring strategies must incorporate temporal dynamics of infiltration events rather than relying on steady-state assumptions, as the episodic nature of vadose zone transport governs actual contaminant delivery to underlying aquifers. Additionally, the air-water interfacial partitioning behavior of PFAS compounds represents a critical mechanism distinguishing long-chain from short-chain transport dynamics in unsaturated soils. This differential interfacial affinity fundamentally governs how these compound classes distribute within soil pore networks and determines their relative mobility toward underlying aquifers. However, the pronounced accumulation of longer-chain compounds at air-water boundaries effectively sequesters these contaminants within the vadose zone, creating a temporary—but potentially reversible—retardation mechanism that does not operate equivalently for short-chain replacements. The relationship between soil-bound PFAS and mobile porewater concentrations remains inadequately characterized for predictive modeling purposes. This measurement gap introduces substantial uncertainty when extrapolating from soil contamination data to groundwater risk assessments, particularly given the chain-length-dependent decoupling between total soil burden and leachable fraction. Specifically, this finding carries significant implications for remediation technology selection: treatments exploiting interfacial phenomena, such as surfactant-enhanced foam fractionation, achieve rapid removal of both compound classes from groundwater—reducing total PFAS concentrations from approximately 15,000 ng L⁻¹ to below 8 ng L⁻¹ detection limits within 15 minutes. The addition of cationic surfactants like CTAB, which exhibit strong air-water interface affinity, enables this rapid extraction from both synthetic and natural groundwater matrices. Furthermore, this disconnect between environmental fate drivers and regulatory classification schemes complicates risk-based prioritization of contaminated sites. The persistence of PFAS compounds—their characterization as \"forever chemicals\" reflecting resistance to natural degradation processes —ensures that vadose zone reservoirs established through historical releases remain active source terms indefinitely, with chain-length-dependent release kinetics governing the temporal pattern of groundwater contamination. The fundamental distinction between chain-length classes in air-water interfacial partitioning has direct implications for how regulatory frameworks classify and manage these contaminants. In addition, current classification schemes for short- and long-chain PFAS derive primarily from bioaccumulation potential rather than subsurface transport behavior, creating a disconnect between environmental fate drivers and risk-based prioritization criteria. This misalignment proves particularly problematic when historical contamination scenarios involve complex AFFF mixtures where transport predictions based solely on bioaccumulation metrics fail to capture vadose zone mobility patterns. Additionally, the environmental ubiquity of PFAS compounds—detected across water, air, soil, fish, wildlife, and human populations nationwide —underscores the necessity of understanding chain-length-specific partitioning for predictive exposure assessment. The characterization of these substances as \"forever chemicals\" reflects their stability and persistence in environmental systems and biological tissues, indicating they do not readily degrade and accumulate over time. This intrinsic persistence ensures that vadose zone reservoirs function as indefinite source terms, with the temporal pattern of groundwater contamination governed by chain-length-dependent release kinetics rather than attenuation through natural degradation processes. Furthermore, the practical implications of these mechanistic understandings extend to treatment technology development. The efficiency of such ex-situ treatments contrasts with the challenge of in-situ vadose zone remediation, where the same interfacial phenomena that enable foam fractionation simultaneously retard natural flushing of long-chain compounds from soil matrices.",

In addition, these quantitative benchmarks enable assessment of whether observed concentrations reflect point-source releases, diffuse regional inputs, or natural attenuation processes. Nevertheless, this spread reflects heterogeneous source distributions, variable transport distances, and differential attenuation processes. The upper bound of this range substantially exceeds typical regulatory screening levels for individual PFAS compounds, indicating that cumulative exposures in affected aquifers present environmental health concerns even when single-analyte concentrations appear modest. The analytical significance of these concentration ranges extends beyond magnitude comparisons. Furthermore, groundwater systems function as integrative reservoirs where PFAS loading reflects time-integrated source inputs modulated by subsurface transport dynamics. Long-chain PFAS exhibit stronger hydrophobic interactions and sorptive affinity to solid phases, theoretically producing attenuated transport distances and elevated concentrations near source zones. However, short-chain homologs possess greater mobility potential, suggesting broader spatial distribution at potentially lower peak concentrations—though aggregated total PFAS measurements limit direct mechanistic interpretation. Establishing true background contamination levels presents methodological challenges. The ubiquity of PFAS in contemporary environmental systems, including atmospheric deposition and agricultural amendments, complicates discrimination between natural background and diffuse anthropogenic inputs. In addition, the lower concentration bound of 22 ng L⁻¹ likely represents a practical detection limit for composite PFAS analysis rather than a genuine geochemical baseline, indicating that even minimally impacted groundwater systems exhibit measurable PFAS burdens. The distinction between point-source contamination plumes and regional background elevation carries profound implications for leaching potential assessment. Furthermore, point-source scenarios, typical of industrial facilities and firefighting training areas, generate steep concentration gradients where long-chain PFAS accumulate near source zones, while short-chain compounds penetrate deeper into aquifer systems. This differential penetration creates characteristic concentration profiles that invert with transport distance—long-chain species declining precipitously while short-chain concentrations persist, ultimately establishing mobile contamination fronts dominated by short-chain homologs. Regional background elevation results from atmospheric transport and deposition coupled with agricultural PFAS applications, producing more uniform concentration distributions where chain-length profiles reflect source signatures rather than transport fractionation. In addition, the 22–718 ng L⁻¹ range encompasses both contamination regimes, with higher values indicating proximity to primary sources and lower values representing diluted or aged contamination. This mechanistic understanding explains why groundwater contamination profiles frequently show short-chain PFAS dominance at distance from source zones. In addition, treatment implications of these concentration baselines are substantial. Documented occurrences of total PFAS at hundreds of nanograms per liter in natural groundwater systems establish that conventional drinking water treatment infrastructure requires reassessment for PFAS-specific removal. The differential treatment challenges posed by short-chain versus long-chain compounds become acute when background concentrations approach regulatory thresholds. In addition, the concentration range data inform risk-based prioritization frameworks. Aquifers exhibiting concentrations toward the upper bound warrant immediate intervention, while lower concentrations may permit monitored strategies—though PFAS persistence fundamentally limits natural attenuation capacity. Moreover, the chain-length composition critically determines appropriate management responses, with short-chain-dominated plumes demanding different treatment technologies than long-chain-enriched source zones. Quantitative baselines serve as essential foundation for analyzing sediment contamination dynamics and groundwater-surface water exchange, providing context for evaluating whether observed concentrations in those compartments represent primary contamination or secondary redistribution from aquifer systems. The temporal dimension of groundwater contamination introduces critical complexity to baseline interpretation. Furthermore, concentration ranges measured at single time points cannot capture the dynamic evolution of PFAS plumes, where source zone depletion, aquifer recharge fluctuations, and seasonal water table variations produce substantial concentration variability. This temporal instability complicates efforts to establish stable background thresholds, as concentrations previously interpreted as baseline may instead represent transient states within evolving contamination scenarios. In addition, the analytical methods employed for PFAS quantification directly influence perceived baseline levels. Detection limits for individual compounds vary substantially across analytical platforms, with some short-chain homologs exhibiting poorer sensitivity than their long-chain counterparts due to ionization inefficiencies in mass spectrometric detection. Reported background concentrations may underrepresent short-chain PFAS contributions when method performance is not uniformly optimized across the full homolog series, potentially skewing chain-length distribution interpretations. Furthermore, pH-dependent speciation of perfluorinated carboxylic acids alters their sorptive behavior, with deprotonated forms exhibiting reduced affinity for mineral surfaces at elevated pH, enhancing transport potential. Dissolved organic carbon competes with PFAS for sorption sites or facilitates co-transport through formation of mobile complexes, particularly affecting long-chain compounds with stronger hydrophobic character. Moreover, these geochemical controls generate site-specific concentration baselines that resist universal application, necessitating aquifer-specific characterization for accurate leaching potential assessment. The Brazilian study context reveals important geographic variability in baseline establishment. Urbanized and agricultural settings present distinct source portfolios compared to industrial point sources, with atmospheric deposition and legacy agricultural PFAS applications contributing to diffuse background elevation. Additionally, this source heterogeneity means that concentration ranges established in one hydrogeologic setting may misrepresent baseline conditions elsewhere, particularly where industrial PFAS manufacturing or intensive aqueous film-forming foam usage has occurred. The 22–718 ng L⁻¹ concentration envelope carries regulatory significance when compared to emerging drinking water standards. In addition, the lower bound approximates detection capability for comprehensive PFAS analysis, while the upper bound substantially exceeds proposed maximum contaminant levels for individual compounds in multiple jurisdictions, indicating that groundwater systems throughout the sampled region require evaluation for potential human exposure. However, regulatory frameworks predominantly target long-chain PFAS, potentially overlooking short-chain contributions that dominate mobile groundwater contamination fronts. This regulatory mismatch between measured concentrations and controlled compounds complicates risk communication and remediation prioritization based on baseline data alone. Air-water interfacial phenomena fundamentally govern the subsurface distribution and leaching potential of chain-length-differentiated PFAS. This differential interfacial partitioning has direct implications for vadose zone transport and groundwater recharge dynamics. Additionally, the pronounced air-water interfacial adsorption of longer-chain compounds restricts their mobility in partially saturated soils, effectively sequestering these species within upper soil horizons and delaying groundwater intrusion. Groundwater baselines in recently recharged aquifers may underrepresent long-chain PFAS contributions relative to their short-chain counterparts, which exhibit diminished interfacial retention and enhanced vertical migration capacity. This mechanistic insight explains observed discrepancies between regional soil PFAS inventories and corresponding groundwater baselines. Consequently, areas with substantial topsoil long-chain PFAS burdens may generate groundwater plumes dominated by short-chain species due to differential leaching kinetics. Compounds classified as \"short-chain\" based on toxicological criteria often exhibit the greatest aquifer penetration, yet receive reduced regulatory scrutiny. Furthermore, this categorical disconnect complicates risk-based management when groundwater baselines reveal short-chain dominance at receptor locations distant from source zones. The persistent environmental distribution of PFAS—encompassing water, air, soil, biota, and human populations—establishes that baseline contamination reflects not merely local source inputs but integrated regional contamination. Their chemical stability ensures that groundwater concentration baselines represent cumulative, time-integrated loading rather than transient contamination events.",
.\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_15\codex_stderr.log:3782:outputs\campaigns\CAMP_20260310T073230_fb78bf\WEB_20260310T073230_5da0fc.json:2893:      "content": "Sediment contamination hotspots function as persistent secondary sources capable of sustaining aquifer recontamination over extended temporal scales. Moreover, these hotspots form preferentially near the sediment surface in zones immediately downstream from point source discharges, creating localized zones of elevated PFAA concentration that persist beyond primary source remediation [10]. In addition, this surface-proximal localization carries particular significance for aquifer vulnerability, as these shallow reservoirs maintain direct hydraulic connectivity with underlying groundwater systems through hyporheic exchange and vertical infiltration pathways—a dynamic explored in detail in the discussion of soil-porewater partitioning mechanisms in unsaturated zones. The secondary source potential of sediment hotspots derives from their capacity to release accumulated PFAS mass following perturbations to prevailing geochemical conditions. Unlike primary sources where contamination inputs occur through direct discharge, secondary release from sediments operates through desorption-driven mechanisms triggered by changes in porewater chemistry, flow regime alterations, or competitive displacement by co-contaminants—processes that parallel the partitioning dynamics governing PFAS mobility in unsaturated soil systems. Moreover, this distinction fundamentally shapes remediation prioritization, as hotspot sediments require active intervention rather than passive source control measures. Long-chain PFAS demonstrate substantially stronger sediment retention and more efficient hotspot formation near discharge points, where these compounds accumulate to higher concentrations within smaller spatial footprints. However, short-chain PFAS demonstrate greater downstream transport capacity, distributing contamination across broader sediment reaches rather than concentrating in discrete hotspots. The bioaccumulation-based classification scheme previously established for PFAS categorization reinforces this functional divergence in environmental partitioning behavior. Long-chain PFAS hotspots represent high-concentration, spatially constrained sources; however, the magnitude of flux to underlying aquifers depends on site-specific hydraulic gradients and mass transfer kinetics rather than concentration alone, reflecting the complex relationship between soil-phase concentrations and groundwater loading documented in soil-porewater partitioning research. In contrast, short-chain PFAS, despite forming less pronounced hotspots, create diffuse contamination zones that sustain chronic low-level loading across extended groundwater capture areas. Neither scenario permits simple risk assessment based solely on total mass calculations; rather, the spatial configuration of contamination relative to groundwater flow paths determines secondary source significance. In addition, treatment implications diverge markedly between hotspot types. Long-chain enriched sediments may respond to targeted extraction technologies leveraging their strong surface affinity. Short-chain contaminated sediments present substantially greater technical challenges given their weak retention across environmental matrices, necessitating more aggressive and costly remediation approaches. However, the differential treatability of hotspot sediments reinforces the economic and technical advantages of preventing long-chain PFAS discharge relative to managing distributed short-chain contamination. Temporal dynamics of hotspot reactivation warrant particular attention in aquifer protection planning. In addition, sediment PFAS reservoirs can maintain release potential over decadal timescales, substantially exceeding the operational lifetimes of typical source control measures. This persistence creates legacy contamination scenarios where aquifer systems continue receiving PFAS loading decades after primary source cessation, complicating attainment of groundwater quality objectives. The chain-length composition of hotspot sediments directly influences this temporal dimension, with long-chain compounds exhibiting slower release kinetics that prolong secondary source activity. Additionally, understanding hotspot formation and secondary source potential provides essential context for interpreting groundwater concentration baselines, particularly in distinguishing true background conditions from legacy contamination signals. Elevated aquifer PFAS concentrations in areas lacking identifiable current sources frequently reflect sediment hotspot reactivation rather than undetected ongoing discharge—a consideration central to establishing defensible groundwater concentration baselines. Furthermore, this recognition shifts monitoring priorities toward sediment characterization and hyporheic zone assessment in groundwater protection strategies. The formation mechanisms underlying hotspot development reveal critical dependencies on sediment physicochemical properties and hydrodynamic conditions that govern PFAS partitioning dynamics. Fine-grained sediment fractions, particularly those rich in organic carbon and iron oxyhydroxides, provide preferential binding sites for long-chain PFAS compounds, concentrating contamination in discrete stratigraphic horizons [10]. However, this grain-size selectivity creates a heterogeneous distribution where contaminant mass concentrates in thin, high-capacity layers rather than distributing uniformly through the sediment column, amplifying local concentration gradients driving diffusive flux to porewater. The hydraulic connectivity between hotspot sediments and underlying aquifers operates through multiple transport regimes that vary substantially with seasonal and climatic conditions. Moreover, during high-flow periods, advective transport through surface-connected preferential flow paths dominates mass transfer, delivering concentrated PFAS pulses to groundwater systems [10]. Low-flow conditions shift dominance toward diffusive exchange across the sediment-water interface, sustaining chronic baseline loading at reduced rates. This regime-dependent transport variability complicates predictive modeling of aquifer loading rates, as empirical measurements at single time points may poorly characterize annual mass loading budgets. Furthermore, the competitive displacement dynamics within hotspots warrant particular analytical attention. Co-occurring inorganic and organic contaminants, including natural organic matter and competing anions, can trigger episodic PFAS release through competitive sorption mechanisms [10]. Moreover, these displacement events, often associated with seasonal organic matter fluxes or anthropogenic chemical inputs, generate transient concentration spikes in porewater that propagate rapidly through connected groundwater pathways. The magnitude of competitive release scales inversely with perfluorinated chain length, reflecting weaker binding affinities for short-chain compounds that render them more susceptible to displacement from sediment binding sites. Temperature fluctuations introduce an additional dimension of hotspot reactivation potential. Nevertheless, elevated temperatures reduce sorption equilibrium constants for PFAS compounds, particularly for longer-chain homologues where hydrophobic interactions contribute substantially to binding free energy. Climate warming scenarios thus project enhanced secondary source activity from existing sediment reservoirs, with thermal sensitivity varying systematically across the PFAS chain-length spectrum. In addition, this temperature-dependent release mechanism intersects with seasonal thermal stratification in surface water bodies, creating depth-variable reactivation patterns within sediment profiles. The spatial configuration of hotspots relative to groundwater extraction infrastructure determines practical exposure consequences. Hotspots positioned within capture zones of municipal or agricultural wells generate sustained contaminant breakthrough curves characterized by extended tailing due to rate-limited desorption kinetics. However, this temporal dispersion contrasts with more immediate contamination signals from primary source proximity, complicating source attribution in groundwater quality investigations. The diagnostic differentiation between ongoing primary discharge and reactivated secondary loading requires multi-tracer approaches incorporating compound-specific isotope analysis and co-contaminant fingerprinting. In addition, sediment hotspot longevity presents fundamental challenges for groundwater remediation timeline planning. The substantial PFAS mass inventories accumulated in near-surface sediments, combined with slow release kinetics, establish multi-decadal contamination trajectories that extend substantially beyond conventional groundwater remediation timeframes. This persistence necessitates adaptive management frameworks incorporating sediment hotspot treatment or containment as integral components of comprehensive aquifer restoration strategies, rather than relying exclusively on groundwater extraction and treatment approaches that address symptoms without eliminating secondary sources. Additionally, the air-water interfacial partitioning behavior of PFAS compounds introduces a critical dimension to hotspot formation dynamics that complements sediment-sorbed reservoirs. In unsaturated soil zones and hyporheic environments where air-water interfaces abound, long-chain compounds partition preferentially into interfacial regions, creating secondary concentration gradients that parallel their sediment-bound hotspots. In addition, this interfacial partitioning effectively sequesters long-chain PFAS in transient, mobile reservoirs distinct from sediment binding sites, generating a dual-reservoir system where contamination exists in both sorbed and interfacial phases. These findings establish that soil concentration measurements alone provide insufficient proxies for groundwater loading potential, as the magnitude of porewater mobilization depends on dynamic equilibrium conditions rather than total mass inventories. Field-scale quantification of PFAS mass transfer between groundwater and surface water systems remains limited, with existing studies addressing this methodological gap through integrated monitoring approaches that capture bidirectional flux dynamics. In addition, this biological endpoint-based categorization aligns with physicochemical partitioning patterns, as compounds exhibiting greater bioaccumulation concurrently demonstrate stronger environmental retention and more pronounced hotspot formation. The convergence of biological and geochemical classification schemes supports predictive risk assessment frameworks that translate molecular structure into environmental fate projections. The convergence of biological and geochemical classification schemes supports predictive risk assessment frameworks that translate molecular structure into environmental fate projections. As a result, PFAS are commonly known as 'forever chemicals' because they do not readily degrade in nature, and are known as forever chemicals because they are stable and persistent in the environment and in the human body, meaning they do not break down and can accumulate over time. PFAS are constantly spreading in the environment and have been found in water, air, soil, fish, wildlife, and humans across the nation. .\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_15\codex_stderr.log:3783:outputs\campaigns\CAMP_20260310T073230_fb78bf\WEB_20260310T073230_5da0fc.json:2908:      "content": "The groundwater-surface water interface constitutes the critical boundary where subsurface PFAS plumes encounter hydrologically connected receptors, yet quantitative characterization of mass transfer across this transition remains substantially incomplete. Additionally, this methodological limitation constrains predictive capabilities for contaminant fate modeling, as field-validated mass transfer coefficients for PFAS compounds remain scarce. In addition, the hyporheic zone serves as the primary locus for bidirectional PFAS flux between groundwater and surface water systems. Hydrological gradients drive advective transport across this interface, while concentration gradients established through differential partitioning mechanisms govern diffusive components. The relative dominance of advection versus diffusion depends on aquifer hydraulic conductivity, stream stage variability, and head differentials between surface and subsurface systems. Moreover, long-chain compounds exhibit pronounced affinity for sedimentary organic carbon, creating concentration discontinuities at the groundwater-surface water transition. Sorptive retention dampens instantaneous flux into surface waters but establishes sustained release conditions where contaminated groundwater discharge occurs over extended timescales, a dynamic elaborated in Section [X.X] regarding soil-porewater partitioning mechanisms in unsaturated zones. Moreover, short-chain PFAS demonstrate weaker sediment binding, facilitating more rapid equilibration across the interface. This reduced retardation enables higher instantaneous mass flux rates per unit concentration gradient, fundamentally altering the temporal dynamics of contaminant delivery to surface receptors. For nationwide distribution patterns of short-chain versus long-chain compound mobility, see Section [X.X]. In addition, temperature and pH fluctuations at the interface modulate sorption equilibrium and transient mass transfer rates, as detailed in Section [X.X] regarding soil-porewater partitioning mechanisms. Moreover, episodic precipitation events generate hydraulic transients that temporarily reverse flow directions across the hyporheic zone, inducing bidirectional contaminant migration that challenges unidirectional transport assumptions in standard exposure models. Surface water discharge fundamentally transforms contaminant fate pathways. Mass entering surface waters becomes subject to more rapid advective distribution across receptor exposure zones compared to groundwater-contained mass. Furthermore, photolytic and biotic transformation processes absent in groundwater may alter PFAS profiles, with the relative persistence of long-chain versus short-chain compounds determining whether surface water discharge represents a terminal sink or temporary transfer mechanism. Treatment system design requires explicit consideration of chain-length-dependent mass transfer kinetics. Additionally, extraction well networks positioned to capture groundwater before surface water discharge must account for the enhanced mobility of short-chain PFAS, which may bypass capture zones designed for strongly retarded long-chain compounds. In-situ treatment barriers face kinetic limitations when short-chain PFAS transit times through reactive media prove insufficient for complete degradation or sorption. The bidirectional nature of hyporheic exchange carries additional complexity for remedial decision-making. In addition, surface water infiltration during high-flow conditions transports PFAS into shallow aquifer zones, effectively expanding the subsurface contamination footprint beyond original source delineations. Coupled with sediment re-suspension dynamics, this creates feedback loops where surface water becomes a secondary source for groundwater contamination rather than solely a receptor. Additionally, quantifying these exchange dynamics through field instrumentation remains essential for parameterizing predictive models. Nested piezometer arrays, seepage meters, and differential flow gauging constitute critical measurement approaches. Current reliance on laboratory-derived partition coefficients and numerical approximations for dispersion characteristics introduces uncertainty that propagates through risk assessment calculations. Additionally, enhanced field characterization targeting chain-length-specific mass transfer rates would substantially improve confidence in exposure forecasts and remedial performance predictions for groundwater-surface water systems impacted by PFAS contamination. Field-based quantification of PFAS mass transfer coefficients remains notably scarce, representing a persistent methodological gap that constrains model validation efforts. In addition, laboratory-derived sorption parameters frequently fail to capture the heterogeneity of natural hyporheic sediments, where organic carbon content varies spatially over orders of magnitude and mineral surface chemistry differs substantially from simplified experimental matrices. Mass transfer rates estimated from batch experiments may deviate from field-scale behavior by factors that remain uncharacterized for most PFAS compounds. The manufacturing facility context examined in recent field investigations demonstrates how source-area hydrogeology governs plume discharge characteristics to receiving waters. Furthermore, high-resolution temporal monitoring reveals that PFAS concentration profiles in discharging groundwater do not mirror static source compositions, indicating that subsurface transformation and differential transport fractionation occur prior to surface water entry. This finding carries significant implications for receptor exposure assessment, as surface water concentrations may poorly reflect original source signatures. Moreover, chain-length-dependent discharge patterns emerge distinctly under field conditions. Long-chain PFAS exhibit pronounced concentration attenuation across the hyporheic zone due to combined sorptive and diffusive limitations, whereas short-chain homologs demonstrate more conservative transport with mass flux rates correlating strongly with groundwater discharge velocity. The resulting compositional shift between groundwater and surface water end-members necessitates separate analytical frameworks for exposure modeling rather than simple dilution calculations. Moreover, seasonal hydrological variability introduces additional complexity for mass transfer quantification. Baseflow conditions favor sustained, low-concentration discharge of long-chain PFAS from sediment storage reservoirs, while stormflow events mobilize short-chain compounds through enhanced groundwater-surface water connectivity and reduced hydraulic residence times. Moreover, this temporal partitioning means that annual mass loading estimates based on single-season sampling campaigns risk substantial bias depending on which hydrological regime dominates the monitoring period. Furthermore, remedial systems designed solely for groundwater capture may prove inadequate when surface water constitutes a secondary contamination pathway, requiring integrated monitoring approaches that track mass movement across both domains simultaneously. Method reporting limits that differ between matrices can create apparent concentration discontinuities that reflect analytical artifacts rather than true mass transfer behavior, complicating coefficient derivation from paired measurements. Additionally, such approaches would enable validation of numerical model formulations currently reliant on assumed equilibrium conditions that may not prevail under dynamic field conditions. Air-water interfacial phenomena introduce additional complexity to PFAS mass transfer dynamics at the groundwater-surface water interface. In addition, this differential interfacial adsorption capacity fundamentally alters transport behavior in systems where entrapped air or fluctuating water tables create extensive air-water boundaries. Long-chain compounds may experience enhanced retardation in unsaturated zone transitions preceding surface water discharge, effectively decoupling their arrival times from groundwater flow velocities. The significance of air-water interfacial adsorption extends beyond unsaturated zone processes. In contrast, recent investigations demonstrated that this mechanism governs the distribution of longer-chain PFAS within soil samples, creating concentration profiles that deviate substantially from predictions based solely on solid-phase sorption. For mass transfer modeling across the groundwater-surface water interface, this implies that long-chain PFAS may exhibit dual-retardation behavior—sorptive partitioning to organic carbon combined with interfacial accumulation—that short-chain compounds largely bypass. Moreover, bioaccumulation potential serves as the definitive criterion for classifying chain-length categories, establishing a functional rather than purely structural distinction between PFAS groups. This classification framework carries direct implications for interfacial mass transfer, as compounds with elevated bioaccumulation potential simultaneously demonstrate stronger interfacial activity and sediment affinity. .\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_15\codex_stderr.log:3784:outputs\campaigns\CAMP_20260310T073230_fb78bf\WEB_20260310T073230_5da0fc.json:2924:      "content": "Indeed, removal efficiencies for long-chain PFAS typically exceed 90%, whereas short-chain PFAS removal reaches only 50% or less under comparable operational conditions [11]. Furthermore, this differential treatment response amplifies the environmental mobility challenges identified throughout this analysis: compounds exhibiting greater subsurface mobility simultaneously resist capture by conventional and advanced treatment technologies, creating a compounding effect with the transport dynamics discussed in Section 'Sediment Contamination Hotspots: Formation and Secondary Release' [11]. The technological gap means that short-chain PFAS, already predisposed to extensive aquifer transport due to weaker soil sorption characteristics as detailed in that same section, encounter fewer effective barriers to long-range migration. The mechanistic basis for this disparity in unenhanced systems resides in surface-active properties governing foam fractionation efficacy. Additionally, as established in Section 'Air-Water Interfacial Dynamics: Different', long-chain PFAS exhibit substantially greater accumulation at air-water interfaces than short-chain analogs, a differential that directly translates to separation performance. Short-chain compounds exhibit markedly diminished surface activity, limiting their transfer to foam fractions and necessitating substantially longer residence times or modified operational parameters to achieve comparable removal [11]. Furthermore, this physicochemical constraint represents a fundamental technological boundary for conventional foam fractionation rather than an engineering optimization challenge. Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) enhancement demonstrates potential pathways for addressing this disparity, as examined in Section 'Remediation Methodologies: Cationic Surfactants and PFAS'. The cationic surfactant operates through dual mechanisms: direct competition for air-water interface occupancy that displaces PFAS molecules, and complexation with anionic PFAS species that modifies their partitioning behavior. Furthermore, this approach acknowledges that short-chain removal requires fundamental modification of separation chemistry rather than incremental parameter adjustment. Field validation in AFFF-impacted natural groundwaters illustrates the performance characteristics of enhanced treatment. In addition, this exceptional bulk reduction performance demonstrates the efficacy of surfactant enhancement for achieving low total concentrations, though compound-specific removal kinetics may vary. The 15-minute timeframe represents optimal laboratory conditions; practical full-scale applications face constraints from groundwater geochemistry variability, competing organic matter, and surfactant dosage optimization that may differentially affect short-chain removal sustainability. Treatment efficiency disparities carry profound implications for aquifer management strategies. Moreover, remediation systems designed around long-chain PFAS behavior will systematically underperform for short-chain contamination, potentially creating false confidence in protective efficacy while mobile short-chain plumes continue expanding. This technological blind spot necessitates compound-specific performance validation rather than reliance on bulk PFAS metrics that obscure differential removal. Additionally, monitoring frameworks must explicitly quantify short-chain species to verify treatment effectiveness, as total PFAS reduction may primarily reflect long-chain capture while short-chain concentrations persist above regulatory thresholds. The convergence of transport and treatment disadvantages for short-chain PFAS demands integrated assessment of leaching potential. Compounds exhibiting 50% or lower removal efficiency enter aquifer systems with reduced attenuation capacity [11], while their intrinsic mobility characteristics enable faster and more extensive plume migration. In addition, this dual disadvantage suggests that short-chain PFAS contamination may represent the dominant long-term groundwater quality challenge even where total contamination burdens are lower than historical long-chain releases. Notably, primary treatment targeting long-chain PFAS removal should be coupled with secondary processes specifically engineered for short-chain capture, potentially including advanced oxidation, ion exchange resins with tailored selectivity, or membrane separations with appropriate molecular weight cutoffs. The economic and operational complexity of such multi-barrier systems underscores the prevention imperative: controlling short-chain PFAS source terms offers greater protective efficacy than reliance on attenuation technologies with inherent performance limitations for mobile compounds. The differential treatment response ultimately reinforces the central thesis regarding differential leaching potential. Moreover, short-chain PFAS combine environmental persistence, subsurface mobility, and technological resistance in a manner that challenges conventional groundwater protection paradigms developed around less mobile contaminants. Effective aquifer management requires explicit recognition that the compounds most difficult to remove from water are simultaneously those most likely to migrate extensively within aquifer systems, necessitating protective strategies that prioritize source control and early interception over reliance on in situ or ex situ treatment efficacy. Moreover, the CTAB enhancement mechanism operates through rapid kinetic partitioning that overcomes the surface activity limitations constraining unenhanced systems. This performance validates that surfactant enhancement fundamentally alters separation chemistry rather than merely optimizing existing parameters. Moreover, the dual mechanism—interface competition and anionic complexation—enables simultaneous capture of both long- and short-chain species that unenhanced systems cannot achieve. However, the 15-minute treatment timeframe reflects controlled laboratory conditions with optimized CTAB dosages and minimal competing matrix constituents. Practical deployment faces substantial constraints from variable groundwater geochemistry, including dissolved organic carbon interference, ionic strength fluctuations, and pH-dependent surfactant speciation that may differentially compromise short-chain removal sustainability. The concentration reduction from 15,000 ng L⁻¹ to <8 ng L⁻¹, while demonstrating exceptional bulk performance, does not guarantee equivalent removal efficiency for individual short-chain compounds whose partitioning behavior varies with perfluorinated chain length and headgroup chemistry. The technological implications extend beyond single-process optimization to systemic treatment architecture. Significantly, the 50% or lower unenhanced removal efficiency for short-chain PFAS establishes a performance floor that conventional engineering cannot breach without chemical enhancement. This constraint positions CTAB-enhanced foam fractionation as a potential primary treatment component, though its effectiveness as a standalone solution remains contingent on sustained surfactant dosing and matrix-specific optimization. In addition, the rapid kinetic achievement of bulk reduction suggests that enhanced foam fractionation may serve effectively as a pretreatment stage concentrating PFAS for subsequent destruction technologies, thereby addressing the volume reduction challenge that limits advanced oxidation process economic viability. Compound-specific removal validation becomes critical given that bulk metrics may obscure persistent short-chain contamination. However, the reduction to <8 ng L⁻¹ total PFAS does not preclude residual short-chain concentrations above regulatory thresholds, as these compounds contribute disproportionately less to total mass while presenting disproportionately greater mobility risk. Additionally, monitoring frameworks must therefore implement speciated analysis rather than total PFAS quantification to verify treatment effectiveness against compound-specific standards. The convergence of rapid enhanced treatment kinetics with inherent unenhanced system limitations underscores the compound-specific nature of technological solutions. Furthermore, the transition from long-chain to short-chain PFAS in industrial formulations, driven by bioaccumulation-based regulatory pressure, has transferred environmental risk to compounds that combine maximum subsurface mobility with minimum technological capturability—characteristics that challenge legacy groundwater protection frameworks developed around less mobile, more treatable contaminants. The evaluation of six cationic surfactants for PFAS removal from groundwater demonstrates that CTAB represents one of multiple chemical enhancement pathways, though its specific performance characteristics relative to alternative surfactants remain subject to comparative validation. This broader surfactant screening suggests that the enhancement approach itself—rather than any single compound—constitutes the critical innovation for addressing short-chain removal limitations. The mechanistic versatility of cationic surfactants, operating through both interface competition and electrostatic complexation, provides a template for future optimization that may yield superior cost-effectiveness or reduced environmental persistence compared to CTAB specifically. The relationship between soil-phase PFAS and mobile porewater concentrations directly informs treatment efficiency disparities at the source-pathway interface. Additionally, research quantifying PFAS mass transfer between soil and porewater reveals that unsaturated zone processes govern the contaminant loading delivered to groundwater systems. Treatment technologies encounter not merely dissolved-phase contamination but dynamically equilibrating systems where soil reservoirs continuously replenish groundwater concentrations. Additionally, this has particular significance for short-chain PFAS: their weaker soil sorption drives higher porewater fractions, increasing the mass flux requiring treatment while simultaneously reducing the efficacy of technologies dependent on interfacial phenomena. The compounding effect intensifies the challenge—greater mobile mass combined with inferior removal efficiency creates a treatment demand that unenhanced systems cannot satisfy. Field-scale groundwater-to-surface water transport studies provide essential context for interpreting treatment limitations within broader hydrological systems. Moreover, quantification of PFAS mass transfer between groundwater and surface water using field data demonstrates that aquifer contamination represents a dynamic source term with continuous export potential. Treatment systems therefore operate against a background of ongoing source contribution rather than static plume conditions. Indeed, this reality underscores the inadequacy of partial removal: 50% short-chain efficiency leaves substantial mobile contamination capable of sustained propagation through aquifer systems and subsequent discharge to surface receptors. The technological gap thus translates directly to persistent environmental exposure risk that end-of-pipe treatment cannot fully mitigate. The regulatory classification basis for chain-length differentiation—bioaccumulation potential rather than groundwater mobility—reveals a fundamental mismatch between environmental protection frameworks and emerging contamination profiles. Moreover, regulatory pressure has driven industrial substitution toward short-chain formulations that evade bioaccumulation-based restrictions while exhibiting maximal subsurface transport potential. This substitution effect positions short-chain PFAS as the dominant groundwater quality concern of coming decades: compounds selected specifically for their environmental mobility encounter treatment technologies structurally incapable of efficient capture. Nevertheless, the temporal dimension proves critical—long-chain PFAS may dominate current contamination burdens, but short-chain compounds will persist longer in aquifer systems due to treatment bypass and sustained source release, ultimately representing the greater long-term management challenge. The environmental persistence of PFAS compounds fundamentally constrains remediation timelines and treatment system design. This intrinsic stability ensures that short-chain PFAS entering aquifer systems through treatment bypass or direct release will persist indefinitely, accumulating management liability across generational timescales. Consequently, the 50% or lower removal efficiency for unenhanced short-chain treatment translates not merely to partial capture but to permanent environmental loading when residual concentrations exceed regulatory thresholds. The national distribution of PFAS contamination underscores the systemic scale of this challenge. This pervasive presence indicates that aquifer systems nationwide function as receiving waters for continuous PFAS inputs, with short-chain compounds particularly favored for long-range transport due to their combination of persistence and mobility. Moreover, air-water interfacial dynamics in unsaturated zones further illuminate the source-pathway mechanisms governing treatment system loading. In contrast, short-chain PFAS exhibit diminished interfacial accumulation, accelerating their transfer to mobile porewater and increasing treatment demand precisely where technological efficacy is lowest. The regulatory framework's bioaccumulation basis for chain-length classification perpetuates this environmental mobility crisis. .\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_15\codex_stderr.log:3785:outputs\campaigns\CAMP_20260310T073230_fb78bf\WEB_20260310T073230_5da0fc.json:3140:      "source_content": "Abstracts\n“Development and Validation of Novel Techniques to Assess Leaching and Mobility of PFAS in Impacted Media” by Dr. Jennifer Guelfo (SERDP Project ER20-1126)\nTo effectively prioritize sites impacted by PFAS for further investigation and/or remediation, standardized tools are needed to rapidly assess PFAS retention, leaching and transport from the source zone to downgradient regions. Standard leaching methods in EPA SW-846, known as the Leaching Environmental Assessment Framework (LEAF; EPA Methods 1313–1316), were originally developed for inorganic constituents and need to be optimized and validated for PFAS. To meet these needs, a primary objective of ER20-1126 is to develop a standard leaching assessment methodology for aqueous film forming foam (AFFF)-impacted media. Methods 1313, 1314 and 1316 have been optimized and demonstrated using four AFFF-impacted soils. This presentation will compare these three methods, discuss approaches to optimizing them for PFAS, and compare the method results and replicability. Consistency between the LEAF methods implemented on AFFF-impacted soils were similar to what has been observed in prior studies for inorganic constituents. Future work will evaluate LEAF estimates of PFAS leaching and mobility to PFAS releases (i.e., leaching source terms) observed in the field.\n“PFAS Leaching at AFFF-Impacted Sites: Insight into Soil-to-Groundwater Ratios” by Dr. Charles Schaefer (ESTCP Project ER20-5088)\nThis ongoing ESTCP project supports the DoD’s efforts to understand the risks associated with the leaching of PFAS from soils that have been impacted with AFFF. Specifically, this research explores the relationship between PFAS concentrations measured in unsaturated soils and PFAS concentrations in mobile porewater that could potentially migrate to underlying groundwater, with the ultimate goal of facilitating site management. The roles of both kinetics and phase equilibria are examined using a field-based approach that uses porous cup suction lysimeters coupled with bench-scale testing on AFFF-impacted soils. This research focuses on assessing the practical limits of lysimetry at AFFF source areas with varying characteristics and developing approaches for selecting appropriate soil cleanup criteria. This presentation investigates the nature of the PFAS in porewater and discusses potential impacts to conceptual site models.\nSpeaker Biographies\nDr. Jennifer Guelfo is an Assistant Professor and an Edward and Linda Faculty Fellow in the Civil, Environmental and Construction Engineering Department at Texas Tech University. She joined Texas Tech University in 2018 following a postdoctoral appointment in the Brown University School of Engineering. For the past 13 years, her research has focused primarily on PFAS occurrence, fate and transport. In addition to academia, she also has a combination of consulting and industry experience, and she uses this background to inform policy and bridge gaps between research and practice. Dr. Guelfo has a bachelor’s degree in geology from the College of Charleston, a master’s degree in environmental science and engineering from the Colorado School of Mines (CSM), and a doctoral degree in hydrologic science and engineering from CSM.\nDr. Charles Schaefer is the Director of CDM Smith’s Research and Testing Laboratory in Bellevue, Washington. He has over 25 years of experience in evaluating the fate, transport and treatment of organic contaminants in water, soil and fractured rock. Dr. Schaefer has served as a principal or co-principal investigator for research projects funded by SERDP, ESTCP, the Air Force Civil Engineering Center, the Navy Environmental Sustainability Development to Integration Program and the Water Research Foundation. He has authored over 90 peer-reviewed publications and has been awarded the SERDP/ESTCP Project of the Year three times as either a principal investigator or co-principal investigator. In addition, Dr. Schaefer has served as the technical lead on several site investigation and remedial efforts supporting state, municipal, industrial and federal clients. He received his undergraduate and doctoral degrees in chemical and biochemical engineering from Rutgers University.",
.\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_15\codex_stderr.log:3786:outputs\campaigns\CAMP_20260310T073230_fb78bf\WEB_20260310T073230_5da0fc.json:3187:      "source_content": "Widespread contamination of PFAS (per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances) threatens public health and the environment. The scale of PFAS contamination is significant, its impact may rival other historic environmental contamination liabilities such as asbestos and PCBs (polychlorinated biphenyls).\nIn October 2021, EPA issued a PFAS Strategic Roadmap, leveraging multiple legal authorities to collect data on the chemicals, impose restrictions on their future use, and to remediate existing contamination. EPA has proposed or finalized rules and released enforcement guidance, accounting for concerns about high compliance costs.\nBecause the science of PFAS is rapidly evolving and improving, the regulatory status of PFAS is currently in a dynamic state; comprehensive PFAS regulation will likely follow an iterative process over the coming years. It is unknown how EPA will proceed with the roadmap under the Trump administration.\n- Introduction – Describes PFAS, the scale of the problem caused by the chemicals, and provides an overview of the Biden administration’s regulatory approach. Also explores the legal questions EPA and other regulators are facing as scientists continue to improve their understanding of the public health and environmental risks of PFAS.\n- CERCLA – Discusses the implications of EPA’s final rule designating two PFAS subtypes as hazardous substances under the Comprehensive Environmental Response, Compensation, and Liability Act (CERCLA).\n- SDWA – Summarizes EPA’s final rule under the Safe Drinking Water Act (SDWA) setting standards for certain PFAS and examine the legal and financial implications for drinking water providers.\n- Other legal authorities – Looks at other legal authorities EPA has used or is using to develop PFAS rulemakings and guidance.\n- Enforcement – Discusses EPA’s enforcement priorities, including the agency’s intention to focus on “significant” polluters.\nIntroduction\nThere are complex legal and policy challenges for regulators to reduce the effects of per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) on public health and the environment. The impact of PFAS contamination has the potential to rival or even surpass other historic environmental health liabilities such as asbestos and PCBs (polychlorinated biphenyls).[1] In this explainer, I describe PFAS, the scale of the problem caused by the chemicals, and provide an overview of the Biden administration’s regulatory approach. I also explore the legal questions EPA and other regulators are facing as scientists continue to improve their understanding of the public health and environmental risks of PFAS.[2] With the second Trump administration, it will be important to see what regulatory actions it may take as well as the responses by industry, states, and the environmental legal community.\nIn 2021, EPA’s PFAS Strategic Roadmap outlined the agency’s approach to mitigate the harms from PFAS exposure through a coordinated approach leveraging multiple legal authorities to collect data on the chemicals, impose restrictions on their future use, and to remediate existing PFAS contamination.[3] Consistent with that roadmap, EPA proposed or finalized PFAS rules consistent with its authority under various environmental statutes, including:\n- Comprehensive Environmental Response Compensation and Liability Act (CERCLA, informally Superfund)\n- Safe Drinking Water Act (SDWA) and Clean Water Act (CWA)\n- Toxic Substances Control Act (TSCA)\n- Resource Conservation and Recovery Act (RCRA)\nDuring the Biden administration, EPA also released enforcement guidance in response to regulated entities’ concerns about high compliance costs potentially resulting from each regulation. EPA’s guidance indicates that it intends to limit its enforcement to significant contributors of PFAS contamination.\nForever Chemicals\nPFAS are a family of per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances, commonly known as “forever chemicals” because they do not readily degrade in nature.[4] They are ubiquitous—found in our soil, water, and air—due to their persistence and widespread commercial use. PFAS have many commercial applications ranging from non-stick cookware to firefighting products and have been manufactured for decades.[5] The hundreds of PFAS substances each have a distinct chemical configuration and name, but their common characteristic of strong carbon-fluorine bonds causes them to be resistant to degradation.[6] As a result, PFAS are constantly spreading in the environment. Scientists have found them in water, air, soil, fish, wildlife, and humans across the nation.[7] Studies have shown the chemicals spreading to remote Arctic and Antarctic sites after decades-long journeys through the biosphere.[8]\nThe first PFAS resulted from the invention of Teflon in 1938, which was used in coatings to resist heat, oil, staining, greases, and water.[9] Today, PFAS are currently used in many types of products, including[10]:\n- Fabrics (nylon, yoga pants, waterproofing);\n- Paints, furniture, adhesives, insulations, carpeting;\n- Food packaging, plastics, mobile phones screens;\n- Firefighting equipment and foams;\n- Cooking materials;\n- Cosmetics and personal care (floss, shampoo, feminine products, lipstick, eye liner, mascara, foundation, concealer, blush, lip balm, and nail polish);\n- Common prescription drugs such as statins and antidepressants.[11]\nSince 2015, as the result of voluntary action, domestic manufacturing of certain PFAS substances has ended.[12] However, PFAS are still imported in bulk into the country in large numbers of products and created as by-products of certain industrial processes. In 2023, the global market for PFAS was estimated at over $28 billion dollars.[13]\nThe Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) estimates that PFAS can remain in the human body for years, and researchers have found that exposure to these chemicals can harm human health.[14] Studies have found that high levels of PFAS contamination are associated with testicular and kidney cancers, children’s development delays, decreases in fertility, liver damage, thyroid disease, ulcerative colitis, obesity, and increases in cholesterol levels and high blood pressure.[15] No “safe” levels of human PFAS exposure have been identified so far, making PFAS regulation more difficult. For example, EPA’s drinking water standards are set at the lowest PFAS detection limits.\nToxicologists and other researchers continue to study the variety of ways PFAS can enter the human body. The uptake of PFAS chemicals by humans through food is a primary PFAS exposure route due to the chemicals’ bioaccumulation up the food chain.[16] Historically, studies have focused on workplace exposures at PFAS manufacturing locations and the ingestion of the chemicals through contaminated food and water near the sites.[17] More recent studies have also found that PFAS can impact humans through dermal contact from clothing, equipment, and personal care products, airborne contamination, indoor dust, carpeting, upholstery, and food packaging.[18]\nThe potential scale of PFAS’s impact on public health and the environment is significant. EPA has identified PFAS contamination at various levels in public drinking water systems serving millions of Americans.[19] EPA also estimates there are over 26,000 sites contaminated with PFAS in the United States, including 126 military bases through their use of firefighting foams.[20]\nEffectively addressing PFAS through remediation, monitoring, filtering drinking water, and replacing the chemicals in materials and consumer products will be a lengthy and expensive undertaking. As with any environmental regulation, the cost of compliance is a concern for industry, but for PFAS the compliance costs will also have implications for local governments.[21]\nMany local drinking water authorities are concerned about the expense of meeting EPA’s federal PFAS drinking water standards.[22] Additionally, importers of products or materials are concerned about how to ensure PFAS do not enter their supply chains and how to comply with EPA’s new tracking and reporting requirements.[23] Entities that used or manufactured PFAS are concerned about the potential broad reach of CERCLA’s liability provisions, especially because of the widespread nature of PFAS contamination.[24]\nEPA’s PFAS Strategic Roadmap\nIn April 2021, EPA embarked on a broad, agency-wide effort to reduce PFAS-related environmental and human health risks, establishing the Council on PFAS, comprised of the leadership of multiple departments within EPA. Recognizing the scope of the challenge of effectively addressing the public health and environmental harms caused by PFAS, the council created the EPA PFAS Strategic Roadmap, a non-binding agency guidance document.[25] The PFAS Strategic Roadmap outlines EPA’s whole-of-agency approach to address PFAS by leveraging multiple agency legal authorities.\nEPA’s PFAS Strategic Roadmap focuses on achieving three objectives using five principals:\nObjectives:\n- Research to improve PFAS detection and better understand the chemical’s impact on human health and the environment;\n- Restrict to prevent further PFAS releases into the air, land, and water; and\n- Remediate to clean up existing PFAS contamination.[26]\nPrincipals:\n- Accounting for the lifecycle of PFAS;\n- Encouraging replacement of PFAS where possible;\n- Holding PFAS polluters accountable;\n- Using science-based decision-making; and\n- Prioritizing protection of disadvantaged communities.[27]\nIn November 2024, EPA released its third annual PFAS Strategic Roadmap report, highlighting the progress EPA has made regarding PFAS in the last three years and reiterating the commitment to its original PFAS objectives and principals.[28] In the report, EPA concludes the progress made under the PFAS Strategic Roadmap “has delivered on essential policies, investments, and research to protect all people and the environment from the harms associated with PFAS” and that its “progress prov",
